missing_perspectives.what: Bill drafted in Putrajaya with no visible consultation mechanism for Borneo states; Merdeka Center 2024 youth survey found 65% of East Malaysian respondents supported equal treatment for all races, the highest pro-equality region.
missing_perspectives.why: Bill framed as national but East Malaysia's demographics, constitutional history, and lived experience of harmony differ from Peninsula.

missing_perspectives.who: Rural Malays in northern and East Coast states
missing_perspectives.what: Merdeka Center 2024 survey found 75% of East Coast respondents rejected equal treatment for all races — reflects economic precarity, low inter-ethnic contact, and dependence on Bumiputera-targeted programmes.
missing_perspectives.why: Sedition Act has genuine constituency among rural voters who see it as shield for constitutional protections.

missing_perspectives.what: ~200,000 people across 18 ethnic subgroups, added to inter-religious harmony committee (JKMPKA) only in 2019. Absent from 2014 NUCC drafts and 2026 JPNIN announcement.
missing_perspectives.why: National harmony commission excluding original peoples is incomplete by design.

missing_perspectives.who: B40 communities across all ethnic groups
missing_perspectives.what: Anwar Ibrahim acknowledged August 2023 that 'those at the lowest feel neglected and thus they will seek out racial and narrow religious issues as a form of salvation.' Pusat Komas Malaysia Racism Report 2024: 73 racist incidents.
missing_perspectives.why: If economic grievance is the fuel for conflict, mediation-based commission without addressing material inequality treats symptoms.

missing_perspectives.what: Malaysia hosts 180,000+ refugees/asylum-seekers (UNHCR), plus stateless populations including stateless Indians, undocumented migrant children, Sabah documentation cases.
missing_perspectives.why: Harmony framework not extending to these populations leaves 'permissible targets' for hate speech.

missing_perspectives.what: Merdeka Center 2024 youth survey (n=1,605): generational divergence on equality. UMNO Youth in 2014 demanded Article 153 protections in any replacement. No youth organisation in 2026 JPNIN stakeholder list.
missing_perspectives.why: Youth will live with this legislation longest.

missing_perspectives.who: Women's organisations and gender-focused civil society
missing_perspectives.what: 2014 NUCC draft Section 7 prohibited discrimination on ground of gender. NCWO endorsed 2018 civil society call. Minister Nancy Shukri stated September 2025 women's participation in peace-building makes peace more durable. 2026 announcement silent on gender provisions.
missing_perspectives.why: If 2026 draft drops gender discrimination prohibition, that is regressive change.

alternative_framings.current: 14-year non-delivery as pattern of political will failure across two PMs.
alternative_framings.alternative: Structural democratic deficit: executive pledges reversed after party assembly pressure, AGC controls drafting without parliamentary oversight, civil society drafts shelved without explanation.
alternative_framings.evidence: 2014 NUCC drafts handed to AGC and PMD but never tabled. 2020 Cabinet cited 'overlapping jurisdiction' with Suhakam and PDRM. Nancy Shukri stated drafts must align with Constitution, religion, culture, government policies and rights of the rulers.

alternative_framings.current: View frames Singapore/Zimbabwe both turning on 'political will'.
alternative_framings.alternative: Singapore's MRHA success may not be replicable in Malaysia because MRHA operates within dominant-party system with near-total executive control, tightly managed media, single island of ~6 million. Malaysia is federal constitutional monarchy of ~34 million with competitive elections, polarised media, two Borneo states with distinct constitutional arrangements.
alternative_framings.evidence: Singapore Parliament passed Maintenance of Racial Harmony Act 2025 unanimously on 4 February 2025 after 5 hours of debate. Malaysia 2026 SKRN Bill being drafted by JPNIN without publicly available draft text.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: India's Communal Violence Bill drafted 2005, redrafted 2011 under UPA; never passed. Faced state government resistance and criticism for presuming majority-community culpability. Remained in cold storage as of 2013.
international_parallels.lesson: National harmony bill drafted at centre can fail when state governments resist.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Pancasila-based interfaith model embedded in 1945 Constitution. Tri-Harmony framework: within religious communities, between religious communities, between communities and government.
international_parallels.lesson: Harmony legislation works best when anchored in widely accepted national ideology.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: TRC 1996-1998 granted amnesty in exchange for full disclosure. As of March 2026, TRC promise 'only partially fulfilled' with government failing to implement recommendations on reparations and prosecutions.
international_parallels.lesson: Reconciliation commission perceived as prioritising amnesty over accountability can lose victim-community trust.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Canada TRC established 2008, issued 94 Calls to Action in 2015. June 2025 Indigenous Watchdog assessment: Canada 'moving backwards' on reconciliation, majority of Calls to Action unimplemented.
international_parallels.lesson: Truth commission recommendations mean little without sustained implementation.

counter_arguments.counter: Absence of ROs under Singapore MRHA since 1992 can be interpreted as evidence of deterrent success, not irrelevance. Minister K. Shanmugam disclosed in February 2025 that government had been prepared to issue ROs but resolved matters through dialogue. 'Threat does the work' actually supports the government's announced preference for 'mediation over punishment'.

NPRC's 10-year mandate was effectively reduced to 5 operational years because enabling Act (Chapter 10:32) was only passed in 2018. Tendai Biti argued commission 'lost five years.' New Gukurahundi Community Engagement Outreach Programme launched, 1,297 submissions received. Failure attributable to delayed enabling legislation and constitutional design, not solely political will.

Sedition Act amended April 2015 to include provisions protecting sanctity of Islam and criminalising calls for secession of Sabah/Sarawak. Retention not merely failure of political will but legislative strategy to add protections. Legislative ratchet — Act becomes harder to repeal with each amendment.

missing_facts.fact: Singapore Parliament passed Maintenance of Racial Harmony Act 2025 (Act 8 of 2025) on 4 February 2025, introducing RO regime for racial content closely modelled on MRHA. Minister K. Shanmugam stated during debate that no restraining orders had been issued under MRHA since enactment in 1990, updating the 2007 parliamentary disclosure.

Zimbabwe NPRC enabling Act only passed 1 January 2018, five years after 2013 constitutional amendment. Commission effectively had 5 operational years. Government now transferring NPRC functions to Zimbabwe Human Rights Commission.
Malaysian Bar called 13 March 2026 for comprehensive National Harmony framework, noting 'harmony cannot be achieved through episodic enforcement alone'.
Suhakam urged May 2025 government to introduce National Harmony Act, citing Pusat Komas Malaysia Racism Report 2024 (73 racist incidents, 517 since 2015).

2014 NUCC produced THREE separate bills, not one: Racial/Religious/Hate Crimes Bill, National Harmony and Reconciliation Bill, National Harmony and Reconciliation Commission Bill. 2026 effort appears to focus on single Commission Bill.

Cabinet agreed 14 August 2020 to establish Inter-Religious Harmony Committee (HARMONI), replacing JKMPKA (2010-2019). HARMONI has 30 members representing major faiths. Exists in parallel to proposed SKRN; 2020 Cabinet decision not to proceed with SKRN cited 'overlapping jurisdiction'.

2015 Sedition Act amendments added provisions protecting sanctity of Islam and criminalising secession calls for Sabah/Sarawak, expanding Act scope beyond colonial-era framing. Made Act harder to repeal because it now carries provisions some stakeholders view as protective.

gender_gaps.missing: Analysis silent on whether 2026 SKRN draft retains gender discrimination prohibition that appeared as Section 7 of 2014 NUCC draft.
gender_gaps.why_matters: If gender provision being dropped in 2026 redraft, represents substantive narrowing of bill's scope.

gender_gaps.missing: Analysis does not consider how women's participation in SKRN commission might affect outcomes. UN Women research: when women participate, agreements 35% more likely to last 15 years.
gender_gaps.why_matters: Composition of commission shapes what harm it recognises.

economic_gaps.missing: Analysis treats racial harmony as standalone domain without connecting to economic inequality. Anwar August 2023: B40 communities 'feel neglected and thus they will seek out racial and narrow religious issues as a form of salvation'.
economic_gaps.why_matters: Economic precarity drives receptivity to racialised politics; mediation-only commission inadequate.

economic_gaps.missing: Analysis does not address how harmony bill interacts with Malaysia's extensive Bumiputera affirmative action architecture. 2014 NUCC explicitly affirmed bills 'will not touch the special position of the Malays as enshrined in Article 153'.
economic_gaps.why_matters: This is the central policy design challenge the bill has faced since 2014.

geographic_gaps.missing: Analysis is entirely KL-Putrajaya-centric. Does not address how SKRN would function in Sabah/Sarawak, where state governments have jurisdiction over land, local government, native law. 2015 Sedition Act amendment criminalising secession advocacy opposed by Sabah/Sarawak MPs. 2026 JPNIN engagement sessions began in Putrajaya; no Kuching/Kota Kinabalu sessions announced.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: National harmony commission designed in Putrajaya with Peninsular race-religion categories risks being irrelevant or resented in East Malaysia.

geographic_gaps.missing: Rural-urban divide within Peninsular Malaysia not considered. Rural Malays in Kelantan/Terengganu more protective of Bumiputera privileges than urban Malays.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Mediation model bypasses informal community leadership structures (penghulu, imam, ketua kampung) that actually resolve most rural disputes.

overall_assessment: Analysis provides disciplined, well-sourced account but substantially incomplete in 5 dimensions: (1) KL-Putrajaya-centric, silent on East Malaysia; (2) omits economic dimension; (3) gender-blind; (4) under-elaborates institutional landscape (HARMONI, Suhakam interventions); (5) international comparator could be strengthened. Completeness 58 reflects significant gaps while acknowledging rigour.
